coredata.dc:title: Constructing a fluorescent probe for specific detection of catechol based on 4-carboxyphenylboronic acid-functionalized carbon dots
coredata.dc:creator: Ye, Qianghua
Yan, Fanyong
Kong, Depeng
Zhang, Jin
Zhou, Xuguang
Xu, Jinxia
Chen, Li
coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots (CDs) were covalently linked to 4-carboxyphenylboronic acid molecules to form the 4-carboxyphenylboronic acid molecules-functionalized CDs (CPBA-CDs). The CPBA functional groups, which were reactive toward vicinal diols, could covalently bridge the catechol with the vicinal diol structures and would cause the fluorescence quenching via the static quenching mechanism. As the concentration of catechol increased, the fluorescent signal gradually decreased. The fluorescent response for catechol detection was in the linear ranges from 0.1 to 56μM with the detection limit of 115nM. We further demonstrated that CPBA-CDs with good biocompatibility were easily and quickly internalized by human umbilical vein endothelial cells and are particularly suitable for multicolour real-time cellular imaging.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
4-Carboxyphenylboronic acid
Fluorescence
Catechol
Imaging

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fluorescence detection of catechol using the CPBA-CDs
Application of CPBA-CDs determination of catechol in real water samples and biomedical imaging

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) UV–vis absorption spectra of CDs, CPBA and CPBA-CDs. Fluorescence emission spectra of CDs (B) and CPBA-CDs (C) with excitation at different wavelengths. (D) The normalized fluorescence emission spectra of CDs and CPBA-CDs.
(A) TEM image of CDs, (B) CPBA-CDs (inset: HRTEM image). The corresponding dots size distribution histogram of (C) CDs and (D) CPBA-CDs.
FT-IR spectra of pristine CDs and CPBA-CDs.
(A) Survey XPS spectra of CPBA-CDs. High-resolution (B) C1s, (C) N1s and (D) B1s region of CPBA-CDs.

(A) Fluorescence spectra of the CPBA-CDs solution with various phenols, (B) fluorescence response of CPBA-CDs to catechol or coexisting substances and the mixture of the coexisting substances with catechol.
Fluorescence intensity change of CPBA-CDs and CPBA-CDs with catechol at different pH.

(A) The fluorescence spectra of CPBA-CDs upon the addition of different concentration of catechol in the range of 0.1–56μM. (B) The plot of fluorescence intensity ratios of F 0/F −1 at 470nm versus the concentration of catechol.
Cell viability of HUVEC after 24h treatment with CPBA-CDs calculated from MTT assay.
CPBA-CDs transfected cells. The samples were observed under bright field (A) and excited at (B) 405, (C) 488, and (D) 543nm. Scale bars: 20μm.
Bright-field transmission image (A) and fluorescence image (B) of PASMC. Bright-field transmission image (C) and fluorescence image (D) of PASMC with catechol exciting at 488nm. Scale bars: 20μm.
Schematic of covalent binding between boronic acid and vicinal diols.

(A) Schematic representation for fabrication of CPBA-CDs and schematic illustration of the proposed nanosensor for catechol based on CPBA-CDs; (B) proposed the static quenching mechanism for recognizing catechol.
Comparison of different sensors for catechol detection.
Determination of catechol in water samples using CPBA-CDs. Relative standard deviation (RSD) is related to 3 measurements.

head.ce:title: Constructing a fluorescent probe for specific detection of catechol based on 4-carboxyphenylboronic acid-functionalized carbon dots

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Separation Membranes and Membrane Processes, School of Environmental and Chemical Engineering, Tianjin Polytechnic University, Tianjin 300387, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Separation Membranes and Membrane Processes, School of Environmental and Chemical Engineering, Tianjin Polytechnic University

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this paper, and should share first authorship.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots (CDs) were covalently linked to 4-carboxyphenylboronic acid molecules to form the 4-carboxyphenylboronic acid molecules-functionalized CDs (CPBA-CDs). The CPBA functional groups, which were reactive toward vicinal diols, could covalently bridge the catechol with the vicinal diol structures and would cause the fluorescence quenching via the static quenching mechanism. As the concentration of catechol increased, the fluorescent signal gradually decreased. The fluorescent response for catechol detection was in the linear ranges from 0.1 to 56μM with the detection limit of 115nM. We further demonstrated that CPBA-CDs with good biocompatibility were easily and quickly internalized by human umbilical vein endothelial cells and are particularly suitable for multicolour real-time cellular imaging.

ce:section.ce:para: Catechol is a typical phenol derivative, which is widely used in the production of hair dyes, photographic developer cosmetics, pesticides, flavoring agents, antioxidant, secondary coloring matters and photography chemicals [1–3]. Catechol exists in environmental samples as pollutants with high toxicity and low degradation in the ecological environment, it is very harmful to human body due to its biotoxicity and irritation to skin [4–6]. Various analytical methods have been developed to detect catechol, including gas chromatography [5], high performance liquid chromatography [7], chemiluminescence [8], ultraviolet spectrometry [9], electrochemical methods [10,11], fluorescence [12] and so on.

The assured methods for the determination of catechol are expensive and time-consuming. Compared to those methods, the fluorescence approach has many advantages including fast response, low cost, high sensitivity, and excellent selectivity simplicity and no need for expensive instruments [13,14]. The derivatives of boronic acid exhibit high sensitivity and selectivity for vicinal diols because of their ability to covalently bond to vicinal diols in basic pH media (Scheme 1 ). Boronic acid reacts with diol leading to the formation of tetrahedral boronate esters. Thus, at pH in the range of 6.5–8.5, the boronic acid exists in trigonal form, but in the presence of vicinal diols, it transforms to tetrahedral anionic form. Furthermore, boronic acid on being attached to a fluorophore, can change the fluorophore's emission [15,16] and the boronic acid group provides an ideal tool for the development of molecular probes for vicinal diols. Liu et al. exploited 3-aminophenyl boronic acid-functionalized CuInS2 quantum dots as a near-infrared fluorescence probe for the determination of dicyandiamide [17] and dopamine [18]. Wu and co-workers [19] synthesized phenylboronic acid modified CdTe/ZnTe/ZnS quantum dots for intracellular glucose detection. Qu et al. [20] synthesized 3-aminobenzene boronic acid functionalized graphene quantum dots and used as a selective and sensitive sensing system for glucose. Quantum dots as fluorophore have some disadvantages such as potential high toxicity and poor biocompatibility. However, CDs are attracting increasing attention because of their excellent physicochemical properties such as the excellent water solubility, strong fluorescence, high biocompatibility and good photostability compared with traditional semiconductor quantum dots [21–24].

According to Scheme 1 and CDs’ excellent properties, we synthesized CDs with amino functional groups from anhydrous citric acid (CA) and diethylenetriamine (DETA) by a hydrothermal approach and prepared 4-carboxyphenylboronic acid (CPBA) functionalized CDs (CPBA-CDs) and CPBA can be covalently linked to the CDs. The CPBA-CDs containing boronic acid functional groups can react with catechol molecules containing vicinal diols to form a large assembly and formed boronate esters, and the fluorescence of CPBA-CDs was quenched via the static quenching mechanism. Therefore, the CPBA-CDs could react with catechol and employed for a highly sensitive and selective fluorescence nanosensor for catechol detection.

Anhydrous citric acid (CA) and diethylenetriamine (DETA) were purchased from the local company. Quinine sulfate (99%, suitable for fluorescence), CPBA, catechol and 3-(4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyltetrazoliu-bromide (MTT) were supplied by Aladdin. All other chemicals were of analytical grade and used as received without any further purification. Double distilled water was used in all experiments.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) measurements were carried out using a JEOL JEM 2011 microscope at an acceleration voltage of 200kV. The absorption spectra were measured on a UV–vis spectrometer (Purkinje, General TU-1901). Fluorescence spectra were obtained by an F-380 fluorescence spectrophotometer. The Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were recorded on a TENSOR37 Fourier transform infrared spectrometer. The XPS spectra measurements were performed on an X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (EDAX, GENESIS 60S). A PHS-3W pH meter was utilized to measure the pH values of aqueous solutions. The fluorescence imaging was observed under an OlympusIX71 inverted fluorescence microscope with a 20× objective lens.

The CDs were typically fabricated by one-pot hydrothermal process as follows according to our previous reports [25,26], a transparent solution containing CA (1.26g) and DETA (1.5mL) were dissolved in distilled water (30mL). Then the solution was transferred to poly(tetrafluoroethylene) autoclave (50mL) and heated at 200°C for 5h (Scheme 2A). After removal solvent by rotary evaporation, the obtained solid was dissolved in ethanol and removed the insoluble precipitate by centrifugation (4000rpm, 15min). The final product was subjected to dialysis (MW=3500Da) in order to obtain the CDs.

0.5g of CPBA was dispersed in 30mL thionyl chloride and this mixture was stirred at 85°C for 12h. After cooled to room temperature, the residual SOCl2 was distilled under reduced pressure to obtain (4-chlorocarbonylphenyl) boronic anhydride which was obtained after complete distillation of the thionyl chloride (Scheme 2A). Then, to the solution of (4-chlorocarbonylphenyl) boronic anhydride in 30mL anhydrous N,N-dimethylformamide was added 0.2mg CDs and stirred at room temperature for 24h (Scheme 2A). After the completion of the reaction, the reaction mixture was concentrated by vacuum distillation. And the residue was washed 5 times with ethanol and centrifuged. The obtained nanoparticles were dried for further use.

The purified CPBA-CDs solution (0.8mg/mL) was placed in a 10mL volumetric flask. Subsequently, different amount of catechol (0.2molL−1) was added. The mixtures were diluted with pH=7.4 PBS (0.01molL−1) to final volume of 10mL. Afterwards, the mixtures were incubated over 30min at the room temperature. Their fluorescence spectra were recorded in the 400–600nm emission wavelength range at an excitation wavelength of 380nm.

The in vitro cytotoxicity was assessed using the MTT assay in human umbilical vein endothelial cells (HUVEC). Cells were seeded in a 96-well plate at 37°C in 5% CO2 for 24h. A cytotoxicity screen was conducted for four CPBA-CDs concentrations: 0, 100, 200, 300 and 400μg/mL. The cellular viability estimated was ca. 96% in 24h after treatment with 400μg/mL of CPBA-CDs, exhibiting low toxicity to HUVEC cells.

HUVEC were cultured in (Invitrogen) supplemented with 10% Fetal Bovine Serum (FBS, Invitrogen) in an atmosphere of 5% CO2 at 37°C. The cells were then incubated with the 200μg/mL CPBA-CDs in the culture medium for 1h, and the medium was removed and the cells washed with PBS 3 times. Finally, 1mL of PBS was added to the plate and the cells were observed under inverted fluorescence microscopy.

CPBA and SOCl2 reaction was obtained (4-chlorocarbonylphenyl) boronic anhydride and the production was covalently linked to CDs in the presence of K2CO3 to form the CPBA-CDs (Scheme 2A). The formation of CPBA-CDs was characterized using the UV–vis and fluorescence spectroscopy (Fig. 1 ). Fig. 1A shows the fluorescence emission spectra of CDs and CPBA-CDs. The UV–vis absorption spectra of CDs, CPBA and CPBA-CDs show an absorption band at 240nm, which is ascribed to π–π* transition [27]. The absorption spectrum of the CPBA-CDs was quite different from CPBA and CDs, which may be result from the effect of functionalization. To further investigate optical properties, fluorescence emission spectra of the CDs and CPBA-CDs are recorded under various excitations. Fig. 1B and C exhibits similar excitation-dependent photoluminescence behavior. This excitation-dependent photoluminescence behavior was extensively reported in fluorescent carbon-based nanomaterials, and it may have resulted from optical selection of differently sized CDs and surface defects of CDs [28,29]. Fig. 1D shows the fluorescence emission spectra of CDs and CPBA-CDs. It can be seen that the fluorescence emission peak of CDs was at 440nm while the emission maximum of the CPBA-CDs was at 470nm. The significantly red shift of 30nm was owing to the change of the surface functional groups which leaded to the extension of surface molecule's chain length and the increase of the final CDs size.

The FTIR assignments are further verified by XPS analysis. Fig. 4A reveals that the CDs comprise carbon, oxygen, nitrogen and boron elements and it shows a B1s peak at about 192eV, a C1s peak at about 284eV, a N1s peak at about 401eV and an O1s peak at about 532eV, respectively [36]. The C1s spectra (Fig. 4B) showed three peaks at 285.2, 285.8 and 288.6eV, respectively, which are attributed to CC/CH, CN and CO bands in CPBA-CDs [37]. The N1s spectrum (Fig. 4C) is fitted with two peaks at 400.6 and 401.5eV, which are ascribed to pyridinic N and pyrrolic N bonds, respectively [33]. The B1s spectrum (Fig. 4D) shows two peaks appear at 192.0 and 192.3eV, which are assigned to BC and BO bonds, respectively [38]. In addition, combined with FTIR spectra and XPS spectra analysis, these results indicated that the NH2 groups on the surface of CDs have reacted with the CPBA molecules. The reaction demonstrated that the CPBA molecules were successfully linked to the surface of the CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence detection of catechol using the CPBA-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Due to that the boronic acid functional groups on the surface of CDs can interact with catechol to form complex compound by coordination, the CPBA-CDs provide a potential possibility to be used as fluorescent probe for phenols detection. Therefore, the selectivity and interference experiments for CPBA-CDs were evaluated to various common coexisting substances such as catechol, resorcine, hydroquinone, bisphenol A, phloroglucinol, nitrophenol and phenol. As Fig. 5A shows these phenols had different influence on the fluorescence intensity of the CPBA-CDs, and the lowest fluorescence intensity was notably achieved for the CPBA-CDs upon the addition of catechol. Fig. 5B shows that the present sensor was not only insensitive to other potentially coexisting substances, but also selective toward catechol in the presence of other potentially coexisting substances.

The pH value is usually important to the fluorescence intensity of fluorescence probes, so the effect of pH on the fluorescence intensity of CPBA-CDs system was firstly investigated. As shown in Fig. 6 , the fluorescence intensity of CPBA-CDs is pH dependent. The fluorescence intensity first increases with a rise of pH from 4.0 to 8.0 and then gradually decreases with a further increase of pH. The highest fluorescence intensity is observed at pH=8.0. However, the fluorescence intensity of CPBA-CDs with catechol was the opposite. The high pH value was favorable for the interaction of boronic acid and catechol, because the formation of boronate complexes was release proton reaction. Considering the above results, pH=7.4 was adopted in the further experiments.

As the increasing of boronic acid functional groups coordinated with catechol, the fluorescence intensity of CPBA-CDs will decrease gradually. For catechol detection study, different concentrations of catechol ions of 0.1–56μM were investigated, and Fig. 7A shows the fluorescence spectra corresponding to the CPBA-CDs systems at different catechol concentrations. The fluorescence intensity of the mixture shows obviously decreases with increasing the catechol concentration. As shown in Scheme 2B, this observation can be attributed to that catechol can quench the fluorescence of CPBA-CDs presumably via reacting with boronic acid group on the CPBA-CDs. The CPBA functional groups, which acted as catechol receptors, could covalently combine with catechol molecule containing two pairs of vicinal diols [17,18]. The quenching mechanism is investigated. The fluorescence quenching of CDs can be divided into the dynamic quenching and static quenching [43]. The static quenching occurs when a non-fluorescent complex of CDs and quencher in the electronic ground state, which must meet the Stern–Volmer. Fluorescence quenching is described by the Stern–Volmer Eq. (1):
(1)
I
0
I
= 1 +
K sv
[ Q ]
I 0 is initial fluorescence intensity in the absence of catechol, I is fluorescence intensity in the presence of catechol, K sv is static quenching constant and [Q] is catechol concentration.

This linear relation I/I 0 and Q indicate the existence of one type of fluorophores in the solution, which is quenched by catechol. From the data presented, the Stern–Volmer constant of CPBA-CDs fluorescence quenching by catechol is calculated to be 0.3913μM and the non-fluorescent complex is formed after CPBA-CDs reacting with catechol. So, the CPBA-CDs are quenched by catechol via the static quenching mechanism. The limit of detection (LOD) was estimated to follow equation: LOD=3σ/m [39], where σ is the standard deviation of the blank sample and m is the slope of the calibration curve. Fig. 7B shows the plot of F 0/F −1 versus catechol concentration in the range from 0.1 to 56μM. The correlation coefficient (R 2) is 0.9956, indicating a very good linear relationship. Based on the results, the LOD of catechol (3σ/m) is determined to be 115nM. It should be noted that the obtained LOD of the CPBA-CDs sensor for catechol detection is lower than those previously reported shown in Table 1 with other different fluorescent probes.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of CPBA-CDs determination of catechol in real water samples and biomedical imaging
ce:section.ce:para: CPBA-CDs can be used as an effective fluorescent probe for catechol, hence, it was used for the determination of catechol in different water samples in environment. We have chosen tap and lake water samples for real sample analysis. Samples spiked with catechol concentration of 2, 5 and 10μM were investigated through the standard addition method. The obtained recoveries of catechol in water samples are summarized in Table 2 . The results indicate that the present method is capable of detecting catechol in practical samples.

As shown in Fig. 8 , we tested the in vitro cytotoxicity of CPBA-CDs and observed the metabolic activity of HUVEC treated with different concentrations of CPBA-CDs. No significant reduction in cell viability was observed for cells treated with CPBA-CDs even at ultrahigh concentrations (up to 400μg/mL), demonstrating that the CPBA-CDs produced by our method are not obviously toxic in vitro.

Taking their wavelength dependent multicolor fluorescence into consideration, CPBA-CDs hold the potential to serve as a multifunctional vector with the ability of bioimaging in HUVEC. As shown in Fig. 9 , the cell internalized CPBA-CDs can be excited by laser with 405, 488 and 543nm excitation, and emitted blue, green and red fluorescent. Furthermore, the combination of different fluorescent images demonstrated that emissive site displayed highly co-localization in the cytoplasm. These results gave us the conclusion that CPBA-CDs were of low cytotoxicity with cells, which agreed well with the results of previous investigation on the interaction of living cells with other CDs. Thus, it can be seen that CPBA-CDs can be effectively applied in cell imaging and biological labeling.

According to bioimaging applications of CPBA-CDs in HUVEC, in view of the above-mentioned higher performance of the sensor CPBA-CDs, bioimaging applications of CPBA-CDs for monitoring of catechol in pulmonary arterial smooth muscle cells (PASMC) were carried out. PASMC were incubated with CPBA-CDs (0.5mg/mL) for 0.5h at 37°C, and then with catechol (100μM) for 10min. As the results, the fluorescence image of the CPBA-CDs without catechol and found that the PASMC exhibited strong green fluorescence exciting by laser with 488nm (Fig. 10A and B). After catechol is added the fluorescence brightness decreases gradually (Fig. 10C and D). The results show that CPBA-CDs can be used to image.

In the present study, 4-carboxyphenylboronic acid functionalized CDs (CPBA-CDs) were utilized as the fluorescence probe for detection and quantification of catechol, which was based on the fluorescence quenching of the probe induced by forming boronate esters with phenol compounds with vicinal diols. The CPBA-CDs exhibit highly sensitive and selective detection behavior of catechol with a low detection limit of 115nM. Comparing with catechol sensing systems reported previously, the proposed method is simple, facile, low cost and has a higher sensitivity and better selectivity. The CPBA-CDs were also used for the bioimaging of human umbilical vein endothelial cells and it has been found the blue, green and red color fluorescence images are possible to capture while excited at different wavelength lights respectively. So, the CPBA-CDs can be useful for the bioimaging application.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The work described in this manuscript was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21374078, 51308390 and 51303132), Tianjin Research Program of Application Foundation and Advanced Technology (15JCYBJC18100 and 15JCTPJC59200) and State Key Laboratory of Separation Membranes and Membrane Processes (Z1-201507).

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Fanyong Yan is an associate professor in the school of Environmental and Chemical Engineering at Tianjin Polytechnic University (PR China). He received his PhD degree from Tianjin University in 2007. His research interests focus on the design and synthesis of molecular probes and sensing materials for temperature-responsive and optical sensors.
